content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

November 2, 1995


Mr. Richard W. Holt
The Prudential California Realty
85 Moraga Way
Orinda, California  94563

                                    Re:  Your Request for Advice;
                                         Our File No. I-95-322

Dear Mr. Holt:

     This is in response to your request for advice under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1  Please note that nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate any conduct which has already taken place.
The Commission provides prospective advice only.  Since your
question does not pertain to a specific upcoming governmental
decision, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.2

                              QUESTIONS

     1.  May you continue to list and market an eight-lot
subdivision which the City of Lafayette Planning Commission
reviewed and approved two years ago?  Will future conflicts of
interest arise with respect to your listing a property for sale as
a real estate agent subsequent to participating in the City of
Lafayette Planning Commission's review or approval of the
property?




_________________________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 CaliRegulations, Sections
18000-18995.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by formal written advice.  (Government Code
Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c)(3).)



     428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-322
Page 2


     2.  Are you disqualified from participating in future
decisions regarding an application to build a health club in the
City of Lafayette and other matters which the developer of the
eight-lot subdivision (the "developer") brings before the Planning
Commission?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     1.  Your question about the Planning Commission's approval of
the eight-lot subdivision two years ago involves past conduct.
The Fair Political Practices Commission does not provide advice
regarding past conduct.

     Generally, as a City of Lafayette Planning Commissioner, you
may not participate in making any decision in which you have a
financial interest. You have a financial interest in a decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on (a) any person or business that has
been a source of income to you of $250 or more during the 12
months preceding the decision, or (b) any real property interest
of yours. Since you are a real estate agent, the broker or
brokerage business with whom you work, the person you represent,
and any person who receives a finder's fee in a real estate
transaction, will be sources of income to you.

     2.  Yes. You must disqualify yourself from decisions
regarding the health club application assuming your home is within
300 feet of property which is part of the application, since the
construction of the health club would have a financial effect on
your property.

                               FACTS

     You have been a member of the City of Lafayette Planning
Commission for 13 years and have served as Chair twice during your
tenure. You have been a real estate broker for about eight years.
You work for Prudential California Realty as a real estate agent,
but do not own any part of the real estate brokerage business.
Your primary business involves sales of already built homes as
opposed to undeveloped property.

     Approximately two years ago, the Planning Commission reviewed
and subsequently approved an eight-lot subdivision in Lafayette.
The developer of the subdivision is a business entity. The
subdivision was approved by the city and the necessary lot
improvements such as roads, curbs, and utilities were made. The
eight lots were then listed with another local real estate broker
for about six months. When that listing expired, the developer
asked if you would be interested in listing the property.

Our File No. A-95-322
Page 3


Before accepting the listing, you asked one of the planning
staff members to check with the City Attorney and the City Manager
to see if either viewed your listing the property subsequent to
Planning Commission approval as a conflict of interest. Although
this was not a formal or written review, it was reported back to
you that since you had not been promised anything by the developer
nor did you anticipate being offered the listing at the time of
the Planning Commission review of the subdivision, there did not
seem to be a conflict. Therefore, you accepted the listing and
have been actively marketing the properties for about three
months. You have not received any payment from the developer yet,
as none of the eight lots have sold. You have, however, spent
considerable time and money marketing them.

The developer recently appeared before-the Planning
Commission representing a client who plans to build a health club
in Lafayette. You have officially disqualified yourself from
these proceedings for two reasons. First, you have a current
business relationship with the developer since you are the listing
agent of the eight-lot subdivision. Second, your home is within
300 feet of property owned by the developer that could conceivably
become part of the health club application.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family, or on:

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

Section 87103(b) and (c).

Our File No. A-95-322
Page 4


     "Income" includes a payment received, including but not
limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent,
proceeds from any sale, or gift. (Section 82030.)

     In real estate transactions, the sources of commission income
to a real estate agent are:

     (A) The broker and brokerage business entity under
whose auspices the agent works;

     (B) The person the agent represents in the
transaction; and

     (C) Any person who receives a finder's or other
referral fee for referring a party to the transaction to
the broker, or who makes a referral pursuant to a
contract with the broker.

                    (Regulation 18704.3(c)(3).)

     Income from the sources listed above is attributable to you
once it is "promised." Income is considered to be promised once a
sale is pending on the property, i.e., the property is in escrow.
Once a sale is pending, all of the parties to the transaction
identified in Regulation 18704.3(c)(3) above, will be considered a
source of income to you for the full value of the commission.
(Regulation 18704.2(d).)

     As a Planning Commissioner, you may not make, participate in
making, or attempt to use your official position to influence a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on a person or business
that is a source of income to you, or on your real property
interests.

     Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is
made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
considered to be reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198.)

     1. Material Financial Effect

     A. Business Entities or Individuals "Directly" Affected by
        the Decision

     To determine when a decision will have a material financial
effect on an economic interest, you must refer to Commission
regulations for guidance. Regulation 18702.1 provides that the

Our File No. A-95-322
Page 5


effect of a decision will be material on any source of income or
business entity, if the entity or source is directly involved in
the decision.

A person or entity is directly involved in a decision before
your agency when that person or entity, either personally or by an
agent:

     (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision
will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or
similar request or;

     (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official
or the official's agency.

     (3) A person or business entity is the subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person or business entity.

               (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

For example, if the developer came before the Planning
Commission regarding one of his own projects, the developer would
be directly involved in a Planning Commission proceeding. If the
developer becomes a source of income to you, you could not
participate in such decisions.

B. Business Entities "Indirectly" Affected by the Decision

You may also have a conflict of interest if a business which
is a source of income to you, such as your brokerage firm or a
business client, is indirectly affected by a decision. Regulation
18702.2 establishes monetary thresholds to determine when a
decision is considered to have a material financial effect on a
business entity. After you have determined the fiscal size of a
business entity, you apply the appropriate standard from the
regulation to determine if a decision will have a material
financial effect on the entity.

For small business entities, Regulation 18702.2 provides that
a decision will have a material financial effect on the entity if:

     (1) The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more; or

     (2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

Our File No. A-95-322
Page 6


(3) The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more.

(Regulation 18702.2(g).)

This regulation may apply to the brokerage firm where you
work, depending on its size, or to a client which is a small
business entity.

C. Real Property "Indirectly" Affected by a Decision

Regulation 18702.3 provides that the effect of a decision is
considered to be material as to real property in which the
official has an ownership interest if the real property is located
within 300 feet of the property which is the subject of the
decision, unless the decision will have no financial effect upon
the official's real property interest.

You must disqualify yourself from future Planning Commission
decisions regarding the application for the health club if your
residence is located within 300 feet of property which is part of
the application because construction of the health club would have
a financial effect on your property.

D. Individuals "Indirectly" Affected by a Decision

If the source of income to you is an individual, Regulation
18702.6 provides that the decision will be material if:

(a) The decision will affect the individual's
income, investments or other intangible or intangible
assets or liabilities (other than real property) by
$1,000 or more; or

(b) The decision will affect the individual's real
property interest in a manner that is considered
material under Section 18702.3 or Section 18702.4.³

For example, if the Planning Commission were to consider a
major development located within 300 feet of the property of an
individual who had just purchased a home from you, that decision
would have a material financial effect on your source of income
and you could not participate in that decision. (Regulation
18702.3(a).)


³ Sections 18702.3 and 18702.4 are Commission regulations which
establish the material financial effect on real property based on
the distance the property is from the property which is the
subject of the decision.

Our File No. A-95-322
Page 7


     I hope this has provided you with some guidance on potential
conflicts of interest.  Copies of the regulations discussed in
this letter, as well as a Guide to the Political Reform Act, are
enclosed for your convenience.  If you have any further questions,
please feel free to contact the Commission's Legal Division at
(916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Hyla P. Wagner
                                        Counsel, Legal Division




Enclosures
